---
title: Local setup
description: Run resq-flow locally, use the preferred topology, and verify that ingest works.
---

`resq-flow` is a local development-only flow visualizer for ResQ telemetry.

It is built around one local contract:

- producers send OTLP to Vector once
- Vector fans out to Victoria and `resq-flow`
- Victoria remains the storage and query source of truth
- `resq-flow` renders a low-latency, flow-oriented live view on top of that stream

## Prerequisites

- Rust `stable` with edition 2024 support
- Bun on your `PATH`
- the shared observability stack from `fullstack`

## Preferred local mode

Use `resq-flow` behind the shared Vector stack by default.

1. Start the shared observability stack from `fullstack`.
2. Start `resq-flow` with `make dev`.
3. Let Vector fan out a filtered copy of traces and logs to `http://host.docker.internal:4200`.
4. Verify endpoints with `make print-endpoints`.
5. Verify live ingest with `make verify-ingest`.

## Development

Run the app:

```bash
make dev
```

Default endpoints:

- relay: `http://localhost:4200`
- UI: `http://localhost:5173`

Helpful commands:

```bash
make test
make build-cli
make test-cli
make print-endpoints
make verify-ingest
make smoke-vector-fanout
make smoke-relay-ingest
make replay
```

## Replay fixture traffic

Use replay when you want quick local flow activity without waiting on a real producer.

Use `make replay` when you want to validate the real live surfaces:

- the browser in live mode
- `resq-flow logs tail`
- flow-scoped or global ad hoc CLI emits alongside fixture traffic

### `make replay`

`make replay` is fixture or demo traffic for local development.

It is useful when you want to:

- validate the live logs view quickly
- validate canvas activity without wiring a producer
- test `resq-flow logs tail` against known sample traffic

Before you run it:

1. start the relay and UI with `make dev`
2. in another terminal, run:

   ```bash
   make replay
   ```

What to expect:

- the live UI should update immediately
- `resq-flow logs tail --flow mail-pipeline` should receive the fixture logs immediately
- this is live fixture traffic, not durable production traffic
- `logs list` only shows it later if the history backend persists and serves corresponding events

## Terminal and agent workflows

If you want to inspect `resq-flow` without relying on the browser, use the CLI.

The CLI is useful for:

- quick relay checks
- bounded recent log inspection
- live log tailing during [replay fixture traffic](#replay-fixture-traffic) or worker debugging
- machine-friendly JSON and JSONL output for scripts and AI agents

The full CLI guide lives in [Using the CLI](/docs/build/cli).

## Direct mode

Direct-to-relay mode still exists for isolated debugging:

- traces: `http://localhost:4200/v1/traces`
- logs: `http://localhost:4200/v1/logs`

Use it only when you intentionally want to bypass the shared Vector path.

## Validation

Standard checks:

```bash
cd relay && cargo fmt
cd relay && cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cd relay && cargo test
cd cli && bun test
cd cli && bun run build
cd ui && bun test
cd ui && bun run build
```
